nodes.prompt: Understanding how your Alzheimer's diagnosis was made helps you grasp what it means. Your diagnosis came from: cognitive testing (memory tests), blood biomarkers (pTau217, pTau181), or brain imaging (MRI/PET). Did you have cognitive or memory testing?

options.text: I'm not sure what tests I had

nodes.prompt: Cognitive testing shows changes in memory and thinking. Modern diagnosis combines this with blood biomarkers (pTau217, pTau181) for strongest confirmation. Did you also have positive biomarker results or brain imaging?

options.text: No biomarkers or imaging, just cognitive testing

nodes.prompt: Blood biomarkers (pTau217, pTau181) show Alzheimer's proteins in your blood. Brain imaging shows physical brain changes. Modern diagnosis ideally includes cognitive testing. Did you also have cognitive or memory testing?

Excellent. Your diagnosis combines cognitive changes with biomarker and/or imaging evidence. This is the modern diagnostic standard for early-stage Alzheimer's disease. Your diagnosis is well-grounded.
nodes.outcome: Cognitive + biomarkers and/or imaging confirmed. Your Alzheimer's diagnosis is well-grounded. Early stage.

nodes.prompt: Your diagnosis is valid but represents one pillar of modern practice. Diagnosis based on cognitive testing alone is valid with an experienced neurologist. Biomarkers and/or imaging alone also suggest Alzheimer's. However, modern best practice combines cognitive testing with blood biomarkers (pTau217, pTau181) for strongest confirmation. Ask your doctor: (1) 'Would biomarker testing help confirm my diagnosis?' (2) 'What is my stage (mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia)?'

options.text: I'll ask my doctor about additional testing

nodes.outcome: Ask your doctor about additional testing for confirmation. Either way, move forward with care planning.

nodes.prompt: It's common to be unsure which tests you had — diagnosis often involves multiple visits. Ask your doctor: (1) 'Did I have cognitive or memory tests?' (2) 'Did I have blood tests for amyloid or tau (pTau217, pTau181)?' (3) 'Did I have brain imaging (MRI or PET)?' (4) 'What do the results mean?' Your Alzheimer's diagnosis means evidence of amyloid accumulation, tau tangles, or both in your brain.

options.text: I'll ask my doctor to explain my tests

nodes.outcome: Ask your doctor to clarify which tests you had and what they showed. Modern diagnosis combines cognitive, biomarker, and imaging evidence.

nodes.prompt: Now that you understand your diagnosis: (1) Ask about disease-modifying therapies (DMTs like lecanemab/Leqembi or donanemab/Kisunla) and your eligibility. (2) Clarify your stage (mild cognitive impairment or mild dementia) and what to expect in 6–12 months. (3) Set up regular follow-up appointments (usually every 3–6 months). (4) Plan: tell family, update primary doctor, ask about legal/financial planning and driving reassessment. (5) Consider a support group or clinical trial. Bring written questions to your next appointment. You're ready.
nodes.outcome: You understand your diagnostic pathway. Focus on disease-modifying therapies, your stage, follow-up, and planning with family and your care team.
